name: Berkelium
name_origin: Named after Berkeley, California, where the University of California, Berkeley laboratory first synthesized it.

discovery.discoverer: Stanley G. Thompson, Albert Ghiorso, Glenn T. Seaborg

occurrence.abundance_summary: Synthetic element not found in nature in measurable quantities; produced by prolonged neutron irradiation of plutonium and curium targets at high-flux reactors.

compounds.name: Berkelium(IV) oxide
compounds.summary: Yellow-brown solid demonstrating the unusually accessible +4 state in berkelium, useful for studying actinide oxidation behavior.

compounds.name: Berkelium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: Yellow-green solid; the most thermodynamically stable berkelium oxide and a typical actinide(III) sesquioxide.

compounds.name: Berkelium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Green crystalline solid used in spectroscopic studies and as a precursor for berkelium metal.

uses: Target material for synthesizing heavier elements, notably tennessine (element 117) which was made by bombarding Bk-249 with calcium-48
Scientific research into actinide chemistry and the limits of the periodic table
reactivity_summary: A silvery, soft, malleable metal that tarnishes in air. It is chemically reactive, dissolving in mineral acids. Berkelium is notable among actinides for the stability of its +4 oxidation state, comparable to terbium's +4 state in the lanthanide series.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Berkelium-247 has the longest half-life (1380 years), but Bk-249 (half-life 330 days) is the most commonly produced and used in research; it can be made in milligram quantities at high-flux reactors.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Oak Ridge National Laboratory - Berkelium-249 production
